title: New
original_prose:
"But I’ve been saved by the grace of Southern charm." 

— Sturgill Simpson, It Ain’t All Flowers



####

[INTERNAL COMMS — SYNRGY PERSONNEL MORALE THREAD // ARCHIVE RECORD 303-A]

FROM: CORP GUARD 2ND CLASS MELINA RAASCHTO: ALL CAMP 09-A STAFFSUBJECT: Re: Thursday Picnic Plans!

Hey y’all! 🌞

Looks like the weather’s holding for Thursday (woo-hoo!). Commander said we can use the East Quadrant if we finish drills early—so bring your game faces and your compliance smiles! 🎯🔥

Games:

Spark Relay (bring your own scanner!)

“Pin the Guilt on the Noncompliant” (Jasper made the blindfolds—so cute)

Duck-Duck-Reclaim (kids love this one!)

I’ll bring my famous Seven-Layer Dip! And yes—still made with real protein, LOL. 😉 (Don’t ask don’t tell, right?)

Can someone bring disposable communion cups? Last batch smelled like burnt ozone.

Compliance & joy, Mel 💅🏻

— This message was flagged for tone recalibration. Please review Camp Morale Guidelines: SECTION 5 — Humor Threshold & Compassion Optics.



####

She didn’t tell me all at once.

We had hours between the canyons. The sky cracked open in soft oranges, and she leaned her head against the truck window like she could still taste the fire behind us.

“Ever been assigned a number?” she asked. Voice quiet. The kind of quiet that knows how to wait.

I didn’t answer. Didn’t need to.

“They gave me 42-Baptiste. Must’ve thought they were clever. Stripped the rest. No Spark ID. No name. Just the designation and a tag around my ankle like livestock.”

She paused. Thumbed something in her lap. Might’ve been one of Juno’s carvings. Might’ve been nothing at all.

“I remember the smell first. Sterilized. Like old bleach and polished chrome. Not death. Not yet. Just the idea of it. They moved me down a concrete hall. No one spoke. Guards didn’t even look at me—not like I was there. Just another faulty diode waiting to be recycled.”

The tires hummed over ruin.

“They didn’t touch me. Not then. Just scanned. Logged. Marched me past the bunkers. You could hear it in the walls—voices singing or crying, couldn’t tell. No words. Just rhythm. Broken rhythm.”

I glanced over. Her eyes were on the road, but her hand was clenched now. Knuckles pale.

“One of ’em whispered ‘signal clarity’ as I passed. Another said, ‘yield rating pending.’ Like that meant something. Like they were preachers delivering liturgy. I think that’s what scared me the most. Not the violence. The... peace of it. The fucking serenity.”

We passed a burned-out checkpoint. A flag still hung from the post—SynRgy’s sigil half-melted, like it had tried to run.

“They took me to processing. Not a room. A cube. No sound. Not even breath. Just white. A screen lowered. Asked for my compliance testimony. I didn’t speak. So it wrote one for me.”

She laughed once. Just air and bitterness.

“Said I’d confessed to gender malfeasance. Said I’d hacked Spark metadata to impersonate a woman. That I’d corrupted two minor vessels with falsified identity protocols.”

My grip tightened on the wheel.

“They printed it. Made me sign. Or I wouldn’t get ‘assigned.’ No assignment meant immediate harvest. They called it ‘recycling upon intake failure.’”

“What did you do?” I asked.

She looked at me for the first time.

“I signed. Then I burned the document with my next breath.”

The wind picked up. Carried ash across the road like snowfall.

“They shaved my head next. Not for hygiene. Symbolism. Clean the diagram, they said. Clean the noise. Then they gave me clothes. Not uniform—scraps. Like an insult. Like we weren’t worth a proper conversion kit.”

She looked down at her boots. Didn’t say anything for a while.

“They didn’t call it a prison. Called it a reclamation lattice. Said we were in resonance correction. The whole camp hummed. Like a song no one wanted to finish.”

I waited.

“They walked me past the chapel. Not like you’d think. Steel frame. No windows. Inside was a Spark rack. Rows of suspended vessels. Still breathing. Not talking. Just—glowing. They used them for calibration. I looked one in the eye. She blinked. Once. Then nothing.”

“What happened next?”

She shrugged. “They put me in with The Choir.”

I’d never heard her say it before. The way her voice softened on that name. Like something holy had cracked open in her mouth and she wasn’t sure if it would bite.

“They thought they were silencing us," she said. "But all they did was put the broken notes in the same key."

She was quiet for a long stretch after that. Just her breath and the hum of the road. But I knew she wasn't done. Not really.

"First night, we weren’t allowed to speak. Mouths taped. They said it was to reduce neural disruption—but really, they just didn’t want to hear us say our names. We whispered them anyway. Not out loud. Just… in our heads. You’d be surprised how loud a name can feel when it’s all you’ve got left."

She shifted in the seat. Just enough to lean her elbow out the window like the wind might scrape something clean.

"They gave us food that wasn’t food. Gel packs. Spark-neutral sustenance. One flavor. Gray. Supposed to dull the tongue, make you forget texture. Juno refused it. Drew her hunger instead. Scratched peaches into the wall with her fingernails until they bled. Said she could taste them better that way."

Her voice was so calm it made me ache.

"Brother Lin prayed every night, even when they buzzed the floor with compression pulses. He whispered gospel through the shocks. One night he grabbed my hand and said, ‘Even if they break the body, the spark sings in silence.’"

She smiled then. A quiet one. Not joy. Just memory.

"Estrella hummed when she braided hair. They let us have some—those of us that passed the yield test. She'd gather strands from the floor, twist them into little cords, hum the same tune every time. It wasn't a song I knew. But it made the room feel… like we weren’t ghosts."

I swallowed hard. She kept going.

"When they dragged her out, she didn't scream. She turned to us and said, 'Remember me whole.' Then she spat at the guard’s boots. Not even defiance. Just punctuation."

She went quiet again. The silence between her words was heavier than the story.

"That night, Juno carved Estrella’s name into the floor under my bunk. I helped her trace it in blood. Mine. Didn’t even hurt. I wanted it there. Wanted something in that place to know she was."

She looked at me then. Not soft.

"They called us unstable. They were right. We weren’t built for diagrams. We were made to break them."

I didn’t know what to say.

So I didn’t.

I just kept driving. And let the firelight in her voice burn a little longer.” she said. “But all they did was put the broken notes in the same key.”



####

She shifted in the seat, legs pulled up under her like the truck wasn’t moving seventy down a spine-cracked highway. Her voice came slow, half-smoked.

"Cellblock H. We called it the Choir. Not because we sang—though some of us did. Because we remembered. And memory has a sound."

She looked out the window. The hills unrolled like a wound.

"They put me there after the designation scan. Said I was a liability. Spark misfire. Gender loop anomaly with unresolved theological conflicts. Translation: I made 'em nervous."

She smiled, just a little.

"First thing I saw was Estrella. She couldn’t have been more than sixteen. Buzzed on one side, curls on the other. Face like defiance had been welded into it. She was humming when I walked in. Not loud. Just enough to remind herself she wasn’t alone."

I didn’t say anything. Just kept the wheel straight.

"Juno was across from her. Never spoke. Never needed to. She carved into the walls with a metal button she smuggled in through her gums. Saints, angels, trees burning from the inside out. She made one of me once—eyes closed, arms up, with wires coming out of my ribs like wings."

Kat’s voice cracked a little.

"Brother Lin had the bottom bunk. He was the first to speak to me. Said, ‘You’re not the only one God misplaced.’ Then handed me a chipped enamel cup of something that tasted like rust and sorrow. Tea, he said. Might’ve been holy water."

The engine rattled. A bird flared out from the roadside and vanished into dust.

"Koji showed up three nights later. Shaved head. Scanned clean. Said he’d been on the inside—spark engineering division, tier three. Faked a psych slip to avoid CORE duty. Told us about the power rigs. Said some were still alive inside the pylons. Just screaming. Forever."

I flinched. She didn’t.

"The first week, we weren’t allowed light. They said darkness would help us forget our distortions. What it did was teach us how to feel each other breathe."

She paused.

"Every night, Estrella sang. Quiet. Just enough. Songs they didn’t catalog. Songs the algorithms couldn’t parse. Spanglish lullabies. Field moans. Gospel run through synth filters and played backward. She had a voice like smoke through broken stained glass."

Kat’s hand moved unconsciously across her thigh. Like she was still counting scars.

"Juno started carving her name into the wall one night. Not Estrella’s real name. Not the one SynRgy gave her either. Just the sound Estrella made when she laughed. It looked like a bird in flight. We all started doing that—carving names that weren’t names. Symbols. Rhythms. The shape of someone’s grief."

I looked at her, but she didn’t meet my eyes.

"Brother Lin kept preaching. Not fire and brimstone. Not compliance theology. Just... memory. He’d quote things no one else remembered. Said he once baptized a child in a storm drain with Spark runoff. Called it divine reclamation."

Her voice lowered.

"They didn’t like that. One night, they pulled him for isolation. When he came back, he had a new name. Designate 17-Pax. He didn’t answer to it. Not once. We made sure to call him Lin. Loud. Every fucking time."

She finally turned her head, met my eyes.

"That’s when I knew they couldn’t win. Not really. You can break bone. You can drain Spark. But you can’t erase the name someone answers to in their sleep."

She settled back against the glass.

"One night, the speakers failed. Whole block went dark. And for the first time, no surveillance. Just twenty-two of us in a corridor meant for silence."

Her fingers tapped out something on the dash. A rhythm. A code. Or a hymn.

"We sang. All of us. At once. Didn’t matter the key. Didn’t matter the words. It was noise, yeah. But holy noise. The kind that makes systems short."

She laughed, but it caught.

"The guards came in swinging. Shock batons. Mute hoods. They dragged out Estrella. Said her yield was too volatile."

I felt her recoil in real time.

"But that’s not this part of the story. That’s the next."

She looked back out the window.

"The Choir. That was our name for it. Because some things can only be survived through sound."

She rested her head against the glass again, but I could tell her mind hadn’t left the Choir.

“Juno didn’t sleep at night. She’d wait until the hum dropped low—right before the spark filters cycled. That was her time. You’d hear it before you saw it. The soft scrape of button against wall. She never looked at what she was carving. Like she didn’t need to. Like it had already been written somewhere else and she was just letting it through.”

“What kind of things did she draw?” I asked, before I could stop myself.

Kat didn’t look at me, but her voice softened.

“She drew me once. Not the way I was. The way I wanted to be. Not tall or beautiful or any of that bullshit—just... real. Present. Like my Spark was wearing me instead of the other way around.”

She paused, then reached down to the pack between her boots. Pulled something small out and turned it over in her palm.

A chipped scrap of ceramic. Blackened. Maybe from a floor tile. Etched into it, rough and precise, was a figure with jagged arms raised like antennae and something coiling through its chest—light, maybe. Or wire. Or fire.

“She gave me this the day before they took her to testing. Never came back. No one ever did.”

She slid it back into her pocket. Like a relic.

“Koji figured out how to time the scans,” she said. “Said the algorithm ran a .77 redundancy loop every 48 hours. During that gap, we could speak, move, even scream if we timed it right. That’s how we built the hymn. Layer by layer. Memory into rhythm. Pattern into survival.”

“What did it sound like?”

Kat looked over, half-laughing.

“Terrible,” she said. “But it made us feel... human. And you know what? That was worse to them than defiance. Humanity.”

She sat up straighter.

“One day, a new girl came in. Maybe fourteen. Couldn’t speak. They’d fried her vocal cords during an ‘exorcism drill.’ Estrella took one look at her and wrapped her arms around the kid like she was made of glass and gold. Didn't say anything. Just held her. You could feel the whole block shift. Like we’d decided—fuck your systems. We’re the family now.”

She wiped something from the corner of her eye. Not a tear. Just dust. Or maybe denial.

“They started punishing us for that. For touch. For song. For being more than inventory. Lin said that was proof. That the resistance was working. He started tattooing verses into his thigh with a needle made from wire. Didn’t even flinch.”

“What happened to him?” I asked, already knowing.

“They took his leg. Said it was a security risk. Too much unauthorized scripture. He laughed as they strapped him down. Told them even amputated saints get remembered.”

I couldn’t speak.

“They didn’t kill us because we were weak,” she said. “They hurt us because we were strong in ways they couldn’t systemize.”

She finally looked at me.

“That’s what The Choir really was. Not music. Not names. Not poetry. Just proof. That even in a place designed to erase you, you could still become something they couldn’t classify.”

Not until the lights of Red Rocks showed faint in the distance like old gods waking up from sleep.



####

“The mornings were worst,” she said. “They always started with the scan.”

She didn’t have to explain. I’d seen the rigs before—neck-braced, eye-locked, copper teeth pressed to the temples like a kiss from something holy. Spark capture. Output analysis. The whole purpose was to quantify belief.

“They lined us up by designation number. Meant nothing, but it kept us in order. There was always static before the hum kicked in. Always a little flicker, like the machines were thinking about letting us go. Then it would hit. Cold. Sharp. You’d feel your Spark lift, like your skin was turning inside out.”

She glanced at her fingers, flexed them like she still expected something to be missing.

“They called it calibration. I called it assault.”

I didn’t argue. Couldn't.

“One morning, the girl next to me collapsed. No warning. She just dropped. Body locked up like she’d been pulled out of socket with no place to land. Her name was Mercy. They said she failed yield compliance.”

“What happened to her?” I asked.

Kat didn’t answer right away.

“They dragged her out. Feet first. You could hear the floor scraping her heels the whole way. Lin tried to speak. One word. 'Enough.' They beat him for it. Took turns. Said he disrupted the feed.”

I gripped the wheel harder.

“They ran indoctrination loops after breakfast,” she continued. “Same twenty-two minutes. Glitched sermon about Spark order and reclamation destiny. Sometimes you’d catch a stutter. Same phrase twice in a row—‘submission refines the vessel, refines the vessel, refines the vessel.’ Like the code forgot itself.”

“What did you do?”

“We learned to blink in time. That’s how we mocked it. Blinks like drumbeats. Estrella started it. Blink, blink, pause. Like morse. Like prayer.”

I almost smiled.

“They added compression drills after that. Neural pulses. Ten-minute surges meant to crush errant signal noise. The pain wasn’t sharp. It was dull. Deep. Like static crawling through your bones, rewriting how you thought about the shape of a chair.”

She exhaled hard.

“Juno tried to draw through one once. Hand cramped up. She kept going anyway. The lines curved wrong, like the metal couldn’t remember what art was. She passed out halfway through. When she woke up, the carving was gone. Sanded out. Smooth as doctrine.”

“What did you do?”

Kat looked at me, finally.

“I stole soap. Mixed it with charcoal from the kitchen burners. We redrew it. Every line. On the underside of the bunk. Took all night. She never said thank you. Just tapped the rhythm against my arm while she traced it with her thumb.”

A pause.

“That’s how she said grace.”

We passed a burned transport truck. Ribs of steel twisted like bone. No one left to bury.

“Estrella started teaching us to pray without speaking,” Kat said. “Said words were too easy to erase. But rhythm? Pattern? Breath? That lives in you. That echoes.”

“How?”

“She tapped your wrist. One-two pause. One. Then waited. You’d match it. Then change it. Then send it back. It was like dancing with your pulse. Like saying: I’m still here, and I see you.”

She rubbed her thumb against her palm.

“One day, Koji brought a broken speaker back from garbage duty. Said it still had enough of a charge to loop a signal. We didn’t know what he planned. He spliced it into the washroom relay. We only heard it once.”

“What was it?”

Kat’s voice dropped.

“A girl. Laughing. Not screaming. Not praying. Just laughing. It echoed down the whole block. The guards panicked. Shut down every relay. Called it a containment breach.”

She closed her eyes.

“But we all knew. That laugh? It was Estrella. It was every last one of us. For five seconds, we weren’t prisoners. We were fucking glorious.”

We didn’t speak for a while.

“The next day,” she said quietly, “they erased everything. Juno’s murals. Lin’s verses. The carvings. Even the floor tags. Like we’d never been there.”

“But you were.”

“We are,” she said. “Because every time I breathe, I hear Estrella in the silence.”

Grace flickered. Not as light. But as rhythm. Something that refused to vanish.



####

“They took Estrella after the hymn,” she said. “Two guards. One with a pulse collar, the other with a scanner that never blinked.”

The air inside the truck went still. Like the story had pulled the oxygen out of it.

“She didn’t resist. Didn’t cry. Just stood and straightened her cuffs like she was walking into church.”

I said nothing. That seemed to be the right answer.

“She was deemed non-yield compliant. ‘Excessive spiritual volatility,’ they called it. Said she hummed through scan pulses. Said she taught a mute girl how to worship without data clearance.”

Her hand was closed around something. I couldn’t see it.

“I begged them. I stood between her and the gate and said take me instead. Said I was louder. Said I’d rewritten protocol on the underside of a dozen bunks. That I knew the sound of broken Spark filters like scripture.”

Her voice cracked. Just once.

“They said no. That her Spark was more accessible. That mine was coded in conflict.”

She looked down at the dash like it might forgive her.

“They let her speak. One sentence. She said: ‘I forgive you for thinking you get to decide what parts of me are real.’ Then she turned to us—just once—and sang.”

“Sang?” I asked, but I knew what she meant.

“Soft. Quiet. Like she was wrapping the block in lullaby. She didn’t flinch when they fixed the collar to her throat. Didn’t stop singing until the sound cut out. Like someone hit mute on God.”

Kat closed her eyes.

“That night, none of us spoke. Not even Lin. We just sat in the dark, each holding something invisible. Juno carved into the silence. Didn’t use the wall. Used my hand. Took a sparrow’s outline and scratched it into my palm with the edge of a chipped toothbrush.”

She opened her fist. Held it palm-up. The scar was still there. Faint. Crooked. Perfect.

“She didn’t say why. But she left it with me. Like she knew I’d carry it out.”

She let the hand fall back to her lap. Quiet again.

“And Lin… he just whispered two words. Like they were gospel. ‘Be witness.’”

I didn’t breathe for a moment.

“That’s how we keep her,” she said.

And I believed her.





####

She didn’t start the story right away.

Just tapped a rhythm on her knee. Something tight. Sharp. Military.

“The next morning,” she said finally, “I woke up and chose violence.”

She turned her head, the firelight in her voice low but rising.

“I’d spent my whole life trying to prove I was real. A real girl. A real fighter. A real vessel. But that night after Estrella—they didn’t leave any room for doubt. I didn’t want to be real anymore. I wanted to be remembered.”

The truck hit a rut, jostled hard. She didn’t flinch.

“Koji started it. He knew the drill schedule. Knew there was a three-minute gap in the corridor loop—right after morning yield pulses, right before indoctrination. He dropped hard. Foamed at the mouth. Even bit his tongue. Made it real.”

She smiled. Something wild in it.

“When the guards ran in, I was already moving. I’d broken a shard off one of Juno’s discarded tiles. Didn’t hesitate. Jammed it right under the first guard’s chin. He gurgled like a leaking pipe.”

I didn’t interrupt. Couldn’t.

“The second one drew fast. Too fast for most. But I wasn’t most. I took his baton. Hit him so hard it cracked. One hit. That’s all it took. I didn’t even breathe. Just moved.”

Her hands flexed.

“They called me unstable. Said I was corrupted. Maybe they were right. But I wasn’t alone.”

The memory lit her from within.

“Lin shouted. ‘The walls remember!’ Then threw a fucking folding chair into the hallway. Juno climbed the bars and scratched the exit code into the control panel with her teeth. I swear to God, Blake—she carved that thing open like it owed her blood.”

She stared forward. The road was vanishing into darkness now.

“We spilled into the halls. Not running. Not screaming. Moving. Like one body. Like purpose wrapped in bone. We didn’t have weapons. Just fists. Teeth. Broken faith. Holy fury.”

I saw it then. Not the prison. Not the Choir.

Her.

“Koji ripped a pipe out of the laundry vent. Swung it like a gospel. Took down three compliance techs. Lin covered the left flank with a mop handle he sharpened on the rec room floor. Juno didn’t stop. Kept carving symbols into the walls as we ran. Prayer graffiti.”

She swallowed hard.

“Some didn’t make it. A girl named Ray—fifteen. Got tagged with a suppressor dart. Folded like paper. Lin tried to carry her. Took two rounds in the leg. He didn’t fall. Not until he saw her Spark flicker out.”

“What did he do?” I whispered.

“He kissed her forehead,” she said. “Then turned around and blocked the hallway with his body. Took five guards with him.”

She went quiet.

“We made it to the service stair. Almost. One of the compliance drones came in hot. Black frame. Four arms. Chemical rig mounted to the spine. It sprayed foam and fire. Juno shoved two of the younger ones behind a wash cart and grabbed the mural chalk she’d kept in her mouth all week.”

“She finished the bird?” I asked.

She nodded.

“On the wall. Big. Bright. With the words under it: We were here. Then she turned and ran straight at the drone. Screaming in a language I didn’t understand.”

I blinked hard. Didn’t stop driving.

“She died standing up,” Kat said. “Didn’t scream. Didn’t burn. Just... stopped.”

“And you?”

“I kept going,” she said. “Pulled two half-dead bodies through that hallway and into the vent shaft. Got them out. Through the main chamber. Into the recycling corridor.”

She took a breath like it hurt.

“We were cornered. Nowhere to run. I had a rusted pole and one good arm. That was it. I braced for the end.”

She looked at me.

“Then the wall exploded.”

“Exploded?”

“Yeah. East quadrant. Boom. Metal and stone everywhere. And through the smoke—Free State rebels. Armor scorched. Masks cracked. One of them had copper wiring braided into his dreadlocks. Flamethrower on his back.”

She sat up straighter.

“He walked right up to me. Looked me dead in the eyes and said: ‘You Baptiste?’”

“What did you say?”

“I didn’t,” she said. “I just asked him: ‘You give a fuck I got a dick?’”

I choked. Then stared.

“What did he say?”

“He shrugged,” she said. “Said: ‘You shoot straight?’”

I knew the answer.

“I said: ‘I do.’”

“And he said: ‘Then I don’t give a fuck.’”

The silence that followed was not empty.

“So I joined ‘em,” she said.

And I could hear it now, beneath her breath. The hymn she never stopped humming.

“I gave up peace that day,” she said. “Chose violence. And I’ll keep choosing it, if it means no more of us go quiet.”





####

We sat in the void again.

Smaller now. Just a stone wide enough for two, but only if neither moved too much. The sword rested at my side, humming low like it missed something.

He was older again. The Child. Not a boy. Not quite a man. Somewhere between memory and shape. Legs crossed, hands folded like he was about to pray—or judge.

He didn’t speak at first. Just stared past me, into the black.

Then:

“She was brave.”

I nodded.

“Not just brave,” he said. “Beautiful. Like lightning that decided it was tired of striking and started building things instead.”

“She was,” I said.

“You loved her.”

“Yes.”

“But not like I do.”

I looked at him. “How do you mean?”

He smiled, wide and soft. “Like a story. Like a hero in a book I wasn’t supposed to read.”

That hit hard.

He tilted his head. “You think she was made for you?”

“No,” I said. “I think I was made to follow her. To write it down. To remember.”

He nodded like he already knew.

“I liked the part with the chalk,” he said. “And the bird.”

“Yeah.”

He looked at the void, then back at me.

“So you fought your way out.”

“Yeah.”

A long silence. Then:

“And then?”

I closed my eyes. Let myself see it again. The canyon. The firelight. Her breath in the blanket beside me.

“Then we had a break.”

“Was it nice?”

I opened my eyes. Found him watching, serious now.

“It was nice,” I said.

And then softer, with something breaking open in my chest: “Yes. The nicest.”
